data.title: Grupo Supervielle: Reducing To 'Hold' On Capital Issues And Lack Of Income
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, I have been bullish on Argentine banks over the past couple of years due to the ongoing liberal reforms in the country that are positive for the banking sector. However, Grupo Supervielle S.A. (SUPV) has not been among my favorite ways to play this theme, and in my personal portfolio I own its peer Banco Macro (BMA) instead. Despite that, I also gave a Buy recommendation on SUPV following President Milei’s win in the midterm elections back in last October, as I was expecting all Argentine banks to benefit from improved investor sentiment after this event.

However, SUPV has underperformed its closest peers over the last year, including Banco Macro, Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL), and Banco BBVA Argentina (BBAR), showing that despite structural reforms in the country that are positive for the banking sector in general, not all banks are poised to benefit from them in the same way.

Indeed, while all banks are expected to benefit from strong credit growth in the country, I saw SUPV as more vulnerable due to a weaker capital position compared to its peers. While the bank has also been able to increase its loan growth at impressive rates over the last couple of years, its capital ratio has declined during this period, and the risk of a capital increase is something that investors shouldn’t overlook, in my opinion.

SUPV’s core equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio was 15.4% at the end of 2025, a recovery from about 13.9% at the end of last June as sovereign bonds increased in value during Q4 2025 following the midterm elections. However, this is significantly lower than its CET1 ratio of 19% at the end of 2024, showing that rapid loan growth has been impacting negatively on the bank’s capital ratio due to higher risk-weighted assets (RWA).

This is justified by very strong loan growth over the past few quarters, with the bank’s loan book at year-end 2025 increasing by 172% compared to March 2024, which is quite impressive and shows that Argentine banks have been pushing somewhat aggressively for credit growth after many years of investing mainly in sovereign bonds. The bank has been pushing especially for commercial loans over the past few quarters, which represented about 63% of its loan book at year-end 2025, while retail loans have declined slightly because the bank’s risk appetite for consumer loans is not that strong.

This background of strong loan growth is a very important factor for the bank’s balance sheet transformation, from being highly exposed to sovereign bonds to a more typical profile of holding loans and securities.

From a funding perspective, loan growth has been supported by deposit growth, which increased by 17% YoY during 2025. Both deposits in local currency and in USD increased by double-digit rates during last year, especially after April 2025, when the bank launched a remunerated account product for payroll and SME customers, leading to higher deposit balances in these accounts. Nevertheless, due to higher loan than deposit growth, the bank’s loans-to-deposits (LtD) ratio increased to 78% in 2025, compared to a level below 70% in the previous year.

This is still a good level in the banking sector, considering that usually an LtD ratio between 95-100% is considered acceptable, showing that SUPV still has some room for credit growth ahead despite its capital constraints. From a funding perspective, loan growth can continue at a higher rate than deposits over the next few quarters, at least until the bank’s LtD ratio remains below 100%, something that I don’t expect the bank to achieve in the next couple of years.

Indeed, SUPV’s outlook is to grow loans by 25-30% in 2026, while deposits should increase by 20-25% during this year. Assuming the bottom growth rate of its guidance both for loans and deposits, this means SUPV’s loan book should increase to about $3.7 billion by year-end 2026, while deposits are expected to grow to nearly $4.5 billion. Therefore, its LtD ratio should be around 81% by year-end 2026, remaining at a very sound level and showing that its credit growth strategy financed by customer deposits is sound.

On the other hand, a higher loan book should lead to increasing RWAs, impacting negatively its capital ratio if its earnings generation is not enough to compensate for higher RWAs. Indeed, this is exactly the case, as the bank’s earnings have been volatile, and SUPV reported a net loss during 2025, while for this year the bank is expecting some improvement. Its guidance is to report a return on equity (ROE) ratio between 4-9%, implying net earnings between $30-70 million in 2026.

As the loan book is expected to increase between $725-875 million (25-30% YoY) during this year, for the bank to maintain its CET1 ratio around 15%, it would need to generate some $108-131 million of earnings during 2026, which doesn’t seem likely. Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, SUPV is estimated to report a net profit of about $75 million, which is above the bank’s own guidance but below what is necessary to maintain a stable capital ratio.

Not surprisingly, SUPV’s guidance is to have a CET1 ratio between 11-13% by year-end 2026, which is not an impressive capital ratio and is below-average compared to its peers. For instance, investors should consider that Banco Macro’s CET1 ratio was around 30% at the end of 2025, as I’ve recently covered in a previous article, being in a much stronger position to both pursue credit growth and return capital to shareholders at the same time.

While SUPV’s capital ratio can be considered somewhat low, investors should be aware that SUPV discloses this ratio to be comparable with other banks, namely in Europe or the U.S., but its capital requirements set by the Argentine regulator are not based on the CET1 ratio.

At year-end 2025, SUPV’s equity amounted to around $750 million, while its capital requirement was about $540 million. While the regulator does not set a minimum CET1 ratio requirement, based on its absolute $540 million capital requirement, this implies a CET1 ratio requirement of 11.1%, which means SUPV currently has some ‘excess capital’ available.

If the bank maintains its CET1 ratio between 11-13% during 2026, as it’s currently expecting, it will remain slightly above the capital requirement, but the buffer is getting smaller, and SUPV clearly needs to retain earnings to maintain its CET1 ratio comfortably above requirements. I think the bank can avoid a capital increase over the next few quarters, but eventually it may have to pursue lower credit expansion if this consumes too much capital, and I think the prospects of capital returns are relatively low, as the "excess capital" is not enough to support both loan growth and significant dividends or share buybacks ahead.

Given this background, I think SUPV will have a much lower capacity to pay attractive dividends to shareholders over the long term, while on a cyclical basis the bank has decided not to pay dividends related to 2025 earnings due to its reported losses during the last year. This is clearly negative for its investment case, as its closest peers are offering dividend yields between 3-7%, with Banco Macro distributing monthly dividends to shareholders. From an income perspective, SUPV is now the worst option among Argentine banks, not boding well for its valuation and potential returns ahead in my opinion.

This backdrop is also reflected in the bank’s discounted valuation to peers, as SUPV is currently trading at about 1.18x book value, while its peers trade between 1.34-1.42x book value. As can be seen in the next graph, the valuation dispersion between Argentine banks is not wide, but SUPV has traded consistently at a discount to peers over the last few months. However, its shares also re-rated considerably following the midterm elections last October, from a bottom valuation of around 0.6-0.7x book value, to a peak level around 1.5-1.6x book value some months ago.

More recently, its valuation has declined to around 1.1-1.2x book value, which is not particularly cheap in my opinion for a bank that has reported losses in 2025 and is not expected to have an ROE ratio above the cost of equity during 2026. Moreover, as the "excess capital" position is expected to be under pressure over the coming quarters and the bank does not currently pay dividends, I think a higher discount compared to peers is reasonable.

Conclusion

While I’m bullish on Argentine banking stocks as the country continues its economic reforms and credit growth is expected to remain quite strong in the near term, which is expected to boost banking revenues and earnings, Grupo Supervielle has a more mixed profile as industry tailwinds are offset by some specific issues.

Its profitability has not been great recently and should not be enough for the bank to maintain an acceptable capital ratio over the coming quarters, raising questions about its capacity in the future to return capital to shareholders, or in a worst-case scenario, it may eventually have to raise capital to maintain its credit expansion strategy.

Given this backdrop and the lack of income in the near term, I think SUPV is not the best choice among Argentine banks, and I see Banco Macro as a stronger player. Thus, I reduce now my recommendation on SUPV to Hold, as the risk-reward proposition doesn’t seem to be great in the short term.

data.title: Kodiak Sciences: Shares Up On Positive Eye Disease Data - But I Remain On Sidelines
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Palo Alto, California based Kodiak Sciences Inc. (KOD) soared in value yesterday, reaching a high of $75 per share (market cap of $2.43bn), as the company announced positive results from its GLOW2 Phase 3 superiority study of Zenkuda (tarcocimab tedromer) in patients with diabetic retinopathy ("DR") - a progressive eye disease caused by damaged blood vessels leaking blood and fluid into the retina.

According to a press release shared by Kodiak yesterday:

Building on the success of GLOW1 and with all patients on a 6-month dosing interval, Zenkuda (tarcocimab tedromer) demonstrated superiority to sham with 62.5% of Zenkuda-treated patients achieving a ≥2-step improvement in diabetic retinopathy severity score (DRSS) compared to 3.3% of sham-treated patients (p<0.0001).

Zenkuda also demonstrated superiority to sham with an 85% risk reduction in the key secondary endpoint of development of sight threatening complications (2.4% with Zenkuda vs 15.8% with sham, p=0.0001) and with a ≥3-step improvement in DRSS (13.7% with Zenkuda vs 0% with sham, p<0.0001).

In its Q3 2025 quarterly report / 10-Q filing, Kodiak states:

Tarcocimab is an investigational anti-VEGF therapy built on Kodiak's proprietary Antibody Biopolymer Conjugate (“ABC”) Platform and is designed to maintain potent and effective drug levels in ocular tissues for longer than existing available agents.

To date, tarcocimab has completed three successful Phase 3 pivotal clinical studies: the Phase 3 GLOW1 study in DR, the Phase 3 BEACON study in RVO (retinal vein occlusion) and the Phase 3 DAYLIGHT study in wet AMD (advanced macular degeneration. In the GLOW1 study, tarcocimab successfully treated DR patients and prevented disease progression with 100% of patients on extended 6-month dosing.

In the BEACON study, in the first 6 months tarcocimab-treated patients were dosed on every 8-week interval (as opposed to every 4-week interval for aflibercept) and in the second 6 months nearly half of tarcocimab patients did not require any treatment while achieving similar vision and anatomical outcomes as the aflibercept group at one year.

In the DAYLIGHT study, tarcocimab demonstrated non-inferior efficacy results and compelling safety and tolerability on a once monthly dosing interval.

As we can see above, the GLOW2 results appear even more compelling than GLOW1, and sets Kodiak up to complete a Biologics License Application ("BLA") to the FDA, requesting commercial approval for Zenkuda / Tarcocimab, likely in Q2 or Q3 2026.

Analysis: Another Positive Readout For A Company Blazing A Comeback Trail

In my last note on Kodiak shared with Seeking Alpha in August last year, I noted that:

Kodiak stock had tumbled from >$160 per share in January 2021, to <$10 per share in February 2022, after KSI-301 - or tarcocimab, missed endpoints in a Phase 2/3 study in the "wet" form of advanced macular degeneration ("AMD") - another degenerative eye disease.

Kodiak stock traded in the $1 - $10 range for all of 2023 and 2024, and the first half of 2025, but having completed a somewhat bewildering array of studies for not only KSI-301 / Tarcocimab / Zenkuda but two other late stage candidates, KSI-501, and KSI-101, management is now eyeing a "series" of BLA submissions in 2026 and 2027, as shown below (source: JP Morgan Healthcare presentation):

As we can see, Kodiak plans to request approval for Zenkuda (to make things clearer, I will stick with this most recent name for the drug going forward) in three different indications, DR, RVO, and Wet AMD, which is something I don't think I have come across in six or seven years covering biotechs, however, data seems to suggest that such a three-fold approval may be more probable than possible.

In these indications, Zenkuda would be taking on the might of Pharma giants Roche (RHHBY) and Regeneron (REGN), whose products Vabysmo and Eylea, and its longer-acting version Eylea HD, earn multibillions of revenues between them - ~$10bn in 2025, in fact.

Vabysmo (faricimab) and Eylea HD (aflibercept) are dosed via injection around every eight or twelve weeks, Kodiak's research indicates - but thanks to a longer half-life, Zenduka offers a less frequent dosing regime, as shown in the slide below:

Furthermore, Kodiak seems to have tweaked the design of its drug (adding free protein and conjugated protein) since its unsuccessful DAYLIGHT study (discussed above), enhancing the immediacy of its therapy, and making for a potentially best-in-class therapy in a ~$15bn market, management believes.

Seemingly, management may wait until Q3 2026, and the reporting of its Phase 3 Wet AMD DAYBREAK study before filing its BLA, meaning there is still one more meaningful hurdle to be overcome - should that data set be positive, the path to approval would surely be clear.

Meanwhile, management suggests that KSI-501, its bispecific drug, "may be even better" than Zenduka. KSI-501 is also being tested in the DAYBREAK Phase 3 study, with enrollment complete, and topline data expected in Q3.

As shown above, Kodiak believes its two assets have superior efficacy and immediacy to "gen 1.5" assets such as Eylea HD and Vabysmo, and can work in tandem with implants, while gene therapies in development may carry to much risk for patients, with long-term safety data unavailable.

Finally, KSI-101 is indicated for macular edema secondary to inflammation ("MESI"), a condition that is currently treated mainly with corticosteroids, which is not always effective and can lead to adverse events for patients.

KSI-101 targets IL-6, a target which has been validated previously for MESI by drug candidates developed by Roche, amongst others, but also targets VEGF. The synergistic effects of the combo may make for a best-in-class product, Kodiak believes, in terms of efficacy and safety, which appears to have been borne out by a Phase 1b study, in which more than half of patients achieved a 15-letter gain by BCVA scoring, with a "well-tolerated" safety profile. Topline data from two Phase three studies is due in 4Q26, and 2Q27, management says.

Analysis: Is Kodiak Still A Buy After Yesterday's Price Surge?

This is the million - or rather billion - dollar question. Kodiak's ~100 page presentation given at the JP Morgan Healthcare conference in January certainly shares a wealth of compelling data for all three of its assets, making it seem likely than, by 2028/29 perhaps, all three could be approved and on the market.

Kodiak in fact announces its Q4 2025 earnings and business updates later today, but at the end of Q3, the company reported a nine-month net loss of $(173m), or $(3.28) per share, a cash position of $72m, and working capital of $72m.

That seems to suggest Kodiak will need to raise a substantial amount of funding to complete its various projects, most importantly, perhaps, securing commercial approval for Zenkuda and starting to earn commercial revenues. As such, I would not be at all surprised if Kodiak announces an at the market fundraising very soon, which will dilute existing investors, and potentially drag the share price down.

Kodiak stock is now up a staggering ~1,300% on a one-year basis, making a mockery of my Hold call issued last October (stock is now up 300% since that note). Frankly, given the size of the market opportunity, if the positive news-flow keeps rolling in, I would not be surprised if the share price doubles again.

There are some caveats to consider however. Multiple other companies - for example Eyepoint Therapeutics (EYPT), Oculis (OCS), Ocular Therapeutics (OCUL), 4D molecular therapeutics (FDMT) and others are advancing candidates similar to Kodiak's through the clinical study process, and therefore long-acting, immediately effective treatments for WetAMD, RVO, and DR may become something of a congested market in the coming years.

It is also not a given that patients will quickly switch from the tried and tested (and remarkably successful) incumbents Eylea HD and Vabysmo - Kodiak may have its work cut out generating awareness of its product, and may have a limited budget for marketing, compared to its Big Pharma rivals.

A potential conundrum also strikes me - if KSI-501 proves superior to Zenkuda in the Phase 3 Wet AMD study, would the FDA potentially ask Kodiak to prioritise its approval over Zenkuda? That would mean that Kodiak has effectively been wasting time and resources on Zenkuda.

In my last note I suggested that the "odds may be narrowing" for an approval of Zenkuda, and today, they certainly seem to have narrowed - only a poor data readout in the Phase 3 Wet-AMD seems likely to derail an approval submission now.

In my last note, I also concluded that:

If Tarcocimab succeeds, I can see the share price spiking upward, but the fact that it would be another 18 months perhaps, and hundreds of millions dollars spent, to secure approval for the drug, by which time it will enter a market crammed with cheap generic versions of Eylea (whose patents are expired), I don't find myself sold on the prospect of sustained growth in the share price.

Nevertheless, if there is any downside share price drift - as is quite common for biotechs as the markets waits impatiently for data - then I would consider a speculative purchase of the stock

That buy-the-dip opportunity did not really present itself, and now that Kodiak is valued at $2.7bn, I continue to see the market opportunity as potentially Kodiak's Achilles Heel, and perhaps its funding situation too (although I'd expect management may address that issues as early as today).

I am not sure the overall value proposition is compelling enough to overcome the incumbents, and wonder if both and Regeneron may be working on their own next-generation assets - it may be revealing that no Big Pharma seems to have considered a buyout of Kodiak, although of course if that does happen, I'd expect a fee of >$5bn may be the going rate.

As such, despite so much data highlighting the promise of all three of Kodiak's assets, I feel that I didn't board this particular train at the right time, and my ongoing concerns around an increasingly congested market not offering enough of a revenue opportunity for Kodiak means I have no option but to uphold my Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Garret Motion: Outlook For 2026 Still Looks Strong
data.description:
I'm continuing coverage of Garrett Motion (GTX) which I initiated coverage on in the beginning of 2026. Refer to that article for more company background. The stock did jump about 18% from $18 to over $21 after my article was published. However, the stock recently pulled back along with the broader market in February and March of 2026.

The company recently reported positive earnings results for Q4 2025. The report provides further insights into Garrett's outlook for 2026 and beyond. GTX's long-term growth outlook still looks strong and the valuation remains attractive.

Insights From The Q4 2025 Earnings Report

Garrett Motion looks solid after its Q4 2025 earnings report. GTX increased revenue by 5.6% yoy to $891 million, beating analysts' estimates by about $16 million. However, EPS declined 13% yoy to $0.40, but beat estimates by $0.02. Garrett's bottom line was negatively impacted by an unfavorable product mix in Q4 2025. This was driven by growth in small engine light vehicle diesel. This was partially offset by growth in commercial vehicles.

The unfavorable product mix led to a decline in the net income margin from 11.8% in Q4 2024 to 9.4% in Q4 2025. This drove the net income down from $100 million to $84 million over the same period. The better news for GTX is that the net income margin did increase from 8.12% for the full year of 2024 up to 8.65% in 2025. Another positive is that GTX's adjusted free cash flow increased 12.6% yoy to $403 million in 2025 over the $358 million from 2024.

The cash flow gain was attributed to GTX's disciplined execution. This allowed Garrett Motion to continue its capital allocation strategy of returning capital to shareholders and strengthening the balance sheet. The company repurchased $208 million of common stock and paid $52 million in dividends in 2025. GTX also repaid $50 million of their term loan in 2025.

GTX was left with less total debt of $1.47 billion at the end of 2025 as compared to $1.53 billion at the end of 2024. Garrett also improved its total cash position by 42% yoy from $125 million in 2024 to $177 million in 2025. The company still has more room for improvement with its balance sheet as GTX has 1.03x more current liabilities than current assets and 1.34x more total liabilities than total assets. This leaves GTX with $802 million in negative equity at the end of 2025 as compared to negative equity of $673 million at the end of 2024.

Garrett has strategies in place for strong execution in 2026. The company's goals include: margin expansion, strong free cash flow, and to increase market share from higher consumer demand. GTX expects to achieve an average battery electric vehicle [BEV] penetration of 19% in 2026. The company also expects a slight recovery of 1.5% for on and off-highway commercial vehicles.

Garrett's 2026 guidance includes mid-point net sales of $3.7 billion, net income of $315 million, and net operating cash flow of $455 million. Garrett's mid-point revenue estimate is in-line with analysts' estimates. This would be just under 4% yoy revenue growth, which looks like a reasonable estimate. The steady flow of contract awards that GTX has been getting should help Garrett achieve this mid-point estimate.

I also see the net income estimate and operating cash flow estimates as attainable since Garrett is likely to experience a favorable shift in product mix in 2026. GTX has been getting contract awards for diesel applications and commercial vehicles which can provide higher margins than smaller engine vehicles.

Key Contract Awards

Garrett received contract awards for diesel applications and for light commercial vehicles and trucks. This is because diesel is valued for its fuel economy, lower emissions, and high torque. The company also received contract awards for commercial vehicles covering on and off-highway and industrial applications. The commercial awards supported Garrett's largest turbo frame size, called the MEG. It also supports after market sales for the MEG product line as a retrofit option.

GTX received a series production award for mobility e-cooling compressors with a China-based truck & bus HVAC supplier. Garrett also began a strategic collaboration with Trane Technologies (TT). This collaboration involves integrating Garrett's next-generation oil-free high speed centrifugal compressors into Trane's commercial HVAC applications. This brings GTX's technology into the HVAC industry.

Trane confirmed that Garrett's solution shows a clear performance benefit vs. the traditional solution. Garrett's testing demonstrated at least 10% energy savings over the traditional solution. This benefit can help attract other HVAC customers to help drive long-term growth. GTX estimates that its e-cooling compressors will scale quickly to comprise over 5% of total revenue by the end of the decade.

Valuation

Garrett Motion is trading at 10.4x expected EPS of $1.77 for 2026. This is below the sector median forward PE of 14.4x. GTX is also trading with a low PEG ratio of 0.63 which is below the sector median of 1.43.

The expected EPS of $1.77 would be a yoy gain of about 16%. This can be driven by analysts' expected revenue growth of about 3.8%. Then, EPS can get a boost from the margin expansion that Garrett expects to achieve in 2026.

The PEG ratio below one shows an attractive low valuation. I like using the PEG ratio for growth stocks as it takes multiple years of expected EPS growth into the equation. GTX's PEG of 0.63 is based on expected average annual EPS growth of 15.8% for the next 3 to 5 years. Garrett's strong expected earnings growth rate has the potential to drive the stock for above-average gains from this low valuation level. The stock has room for PE expansion.

Technical Perspective

Garrett Motion's weekly chart above shows the stock recovering from the recent pullback. The RSI held above the 50 level during this pullback which is bullish for the stock. The MACD is currently in bearish mode as the blue line crossed below the red signal line. The MACD is really just reflecting the price action of the pullback.

The recent pullback just looks like another bull flag formation after the bull flag from October 2025 to November 2025. The more recent bull flag just had a sharper pullback than the last one. The latest pullback was likely due to profit-taking and from the broader market's pullback in February and March.

With continued positive news through 2026, GTX's stock could continue higher and make a new all-time high. News of new lucrative contract awards are possible positive catalysts. The next earnings report which is estimated to be between April 23, 2026 and April 30, 2026 could also act as a positive catalyst. Analysts are expecting revenue of $919 million and normalized EPS of $0.41 for Q1 2026. The stock could respond favorably to positive earnings results and from upbeat guidance.

The Risks for Garrett Motion

The company's sales are concentrated among a few large customers. Sixty-two percent of GTX's net sales came from their top ten customers in 2025. The largest customer comprised 12% of net sales. This high concentration of revenue puts Garrett at risk. Loss of business from one or more of the top customers could have a significant negative impact on GTX's revenue and earnings.

Garrett could be at risk for geopolitical conflicts. For example, the conflict between the United States/Israel and Iran has been driving the price of oil and gasoline higher. The higher fuel prices could reduce the demand for discretionary spending by consumers and businesses. This could reduce or delay new contract awards for Garrett.

Competition remains another risk for Garrett Motion. GTX competes internationally with other manufacturers of turbochargers and zero-emission solutions. This includes large national companies and small regional firms. Increased competition could take market share away from Garrett and make conditions more difficult for the company to achieve its strong growth expectations.

Garrett's balance sheet could use further strengthening. The company is still running with negative total equity. The company's current and total liabilities exceed current and total assets. I typically like to see companies with more assets than liabilities. However, Garrett can continue to strengthen its balance sheet as the company continues to grow.

Garrett Motion's Long-Term Outlook

Garrett's long-term growth outlook looks positive. The company is poised to benefit from the auto industry's increasing use of turbochargers to increase fuel economy while maintaining sufficient horse power. The company's e-cooling compressors represent another growth opportunity for the longer-term.

The stock has the potential to outperform the broader market in 2026 due to GTX's strong expected earnings growth from the low valuation level. GTX's earnings expectations of 16% for 2026 are in-line with the S&P 500's expectations of about 16% earnings growth (according to analysts).

GTX could outperform in 2026 with the potential for PE expansion from the low valuation level. GTX's forward PE of 10.4x deserves to be trading closer to the sector median of 14.5x due to the company's strong growth. If the PE increased to 14.4x, the stock price would hit $25 to $26 based on the expected EPS of $1.77 for 2026.

data.title: KRBN: Updated Thoughts On Global Carbon Allowances
data.description:
The performance of the Krane Shares Global Carbon ETF (KRBN) is roughly flat since I recommended it in July 2025. That article explains how the fund works and its objectives. The key for my investment thesis with this ETF is not a stand alone bet the price of the ETF will go up but that it provides for excellent portfolio diversification and a hedge against rising carbon costs for industrial assets, especially useful if you are very long energy investments like me. That said, despite likely short term headwinds for this ETF and its underlying investments, the longer term picture remains solid.

The KRBN ETF is an index tracker fund. It tracks the S&P Global Carbon Credit Index.

This is an index of the world's major carbon futures markets. According to Krane Shares the index weightings are: European Union Allowances with a weight of 60%, California Carbon Allowances with a weight of 25%, and 5% weightings in each of the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, UK Allowances, and Washington Carbon Allowances. The exact methodology of the S&P Global Carbon Credit Index can be found here.

According to the index methodology, the objective of the index is as follows:

The S&P Global Carbon Credit Indices measure the performance of carbon credits traded on global Compliance Carbon Markets. The indices apply capping on a regional basis into EMEA, the Americas, and APAC jurisdictions, as well as on a program-level basis within each region to ensure diversification.

Carbon credit futures must meet all the following criteria: accessibility of futures markets, liquidity and contract selection and carbon credit program maturity. The index has a maximum weight of 65% for given geographical region, and a minimum weight of 5% for any given carbon credit program. Because the EU Emissions Trading System is by far the biggest by volume and most mature market the European Union Allowance take up 60% of the index weighting. The weights will shift slightly as essentially you can think of the index as a market cap weighted index, but it is likely that European Union Allowances and California Carbon Allowances will take up the lion's share of the index, given their respective sizes, and the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, UK Allowances, and Washington Carbon Allowances will stay at 5% each.

As these are futures markets, KRBN holds futures on margin, and then cash collateral for the balance, which is invested in money market instruments and returns a cash like yield. According to Krane Shares, KRBN's "Holdings, carbon allowance exposures, and collateral are subject to change."

Recently S&P Global held a webinar on carbon markets. So I thought this webinar would be worth taking note of. Here are my notes from what the S&P experts said.

European Union Allowances - EU Emissions Trading System

Once ETS1 and ETS2 (starting in 2028) are running together, they will cover about 80% of European Union emissions, making the ETS the largest carbon market by value. Between April 2025 and mid-January, carbon prices were steadily increasing from 60s to 90s (Euros per ton), driven by investment funds building long positions. Several countries started calling for reforms to the ETS to protect European industry and competitiveness, causing the prices to fall back to 60s. S&P analysts expect ETS emissions to decline by about 24% and the allowance supply to fall by about 36% during the 2025-2030 phase.

Oversupply of allowances falls from about 1.1 billion allowances today to about 457 million by 2030, after which things become less clear. The rules for the next phase have not yet been legislated, so we need to make assumptions about several key parameters. Today the Linear Reduction Factor (the fixed annual rate at which the overall emissions cap is reduced each year) stands at 4.3%, rising to 4.4% from 2028. In S&P analysts' base case, a much lower Linear Reduction Factor of 1.75% is assumed post-2030, and it’s important to note that under this assumption the system does not reach net zero by 2050.

It is possible there could be a role for carbon removals and we can expect domestic carbon removals to be integrated into the ETS. There is active discussion about changing the Market Stability Reserve amount: either increasing it from the current 400 million tonnes or even cancelling the threshold entirely. The other active discussion around the Market Stability Reserve is the intake rate — right now it's 24%, and there's discussion about reducing it to 12% in the next phase.

All in all, 2026 will be a key year for the EU Emissions Trading System as the ‘2026 EU ETS review' kicks off. According to LSEG Carbon Research "The review will also act as the primary checkpoint ahead of the transition to Phase V of the EU ETS which begins in 2031."

Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative - RGGI

As a reminder RGGI covers Northeast US Power.

The data from 2021 to 2025 highlights year-to-year fluctuations influenced by fuel switching, power demand, and facility-level changes. RGGI is progressing towards a tighter cap through 2033.

Comparing control period 5 (2021-2023) and 2024-2026 (period 6), one can see total regional emissions staying elevated despite Virginia exiting the market after 2023, as rising emissions from New York and other states compensated for Virginia's removal.

RGGI pricing is heavily influenced by scarcity and regulatory tightening, with such market mechanisms in place: the price floor and the Cost Containment Reserve (adds structured responses needed for price spikes). Scarcity prices climbed steadily as auctions reveal tightening supply and increasing cost containment reserve activity: auction of 2026 Q1 saw clearing price at $24.99 exceeding the cost containment reserve trigger of $18.22, causing all the cost containment reserve volume (7.85 million allowances) to be released.

Anticipating future scarcity, entities tend to front-load purchases, raising bid pressure in the year’s first auction. RGGI's third programme review tightens the allowance budget for 2027-2033, expands the cost containment reserve into tiers, and eliminates offsets entirely. Market sentiment is still cautious with stable open interest since the end of 2025 masking a bearish tilt.

CFTC Commitments of Traders data shows compliance and speculative players are both net short, while swap dealers and market makers hold net long positions.

California Carbon Allowances and Western Climate Initiative (California–Quebec)

In 2025, market prices remained low due to regulatory uncertainty and unclear future allowance supply, with both current and advance allowance prices at floor price of $27.94 in Q1 2026 auction.

The outcome was bearish at the start of 2026, due to ongoing delays in California Air Resources Board's Initial Statement of Reasons rulemaking, expected in May (staff release issued in January). In Q4 2025 future vintage 2028 allowances cleared at slightly higher than 2025 prices.

Transitioning from a $1.27 premium (Q1 2025) to a $1.29 discount (Q4) indicates investors anticipating more constrained conditions and elevated prices going into the future. In California Carbon Allowances futures, forward price curves were up in contrast to the current vintage, reflecting underlying confidence regarding future periods despite weak spot prices.

There was also a net long position from speculative and covered entities, reflecting bullish sentiment from future traders anticipating price gains. Once the Initial Statement of Reasons final rule-making is in place, it will remove 118 million allowances during 2027-2030 and cause a structural bullish shift, while raising medium- and long-term California Carbon Allowances price expectations.

Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS)

According to California Air Resources Board "The Low Carbon Fuel Standard is designed to decrease the carbon intensity of California's transportation fuel pool and provide an increasing range of low-carbon and renewable alternatives, which reduce petroleum dependency and achieve air quality benefits." Because of this it's an important initiative that impacts California Carbon Allowances prices.

California’s market is undergoing structural adjustment, with new amendments tightening carbon intensity reduction targets and increasing compliance pressure. While the market has been grappling with credit oversupply, the tightening has recently shown the deficit beginning to exceed credit generation, which will shrink the credit bank and lift prices amid more stringent carbon intensity requirements.

West Coast LCFS markets experienced significant price volatility in 2025. California LCFS prices (mid-$60 range for most of 2025) were suppressed by the surplus credit bank. Whereas, the Oregon Clean Fuel Programme saw a credit surge of over three times, going from $40 to $150 in 2025.

The California Air Resources Board, approved more aggressive LCFS amendments, imposing a 9% carbon intensity reduction in 2025, rising to 30% in 2030 and 90% by 2045, to draw down the credit bank and firm up prices.

Washington is harmonizing with California's LCFS to simplify cross-state compliance. In the US, the Renewable Fuel Standard is still a standalone policy, with no policy overlap for renewable fuel programs with the compliance markets. Biofuel and RIN prices are important to watch to see how they're impacting the LCFS.

That's it for my notes on the S&P Global Carbon Markets webinar. Overall, it seems to me that in the near term we could see further price weakness in KRBN, driven by the EU ETS component. According to Allianz,

The conflict in Iran and associated energy price and inflation expectations have additionally spurred proposals to use the [EU] ETS as a lever for short-term price relief.

The Emissions Emissions Trading System is like a tax on industry, and the political pressure is to ease this up in the near term to help Europe's competitiveness. At least while energy prices are high. Longer term, it seems to me however, that Europe is committed to emissions reductions and will continue to reduce free allowances to industry and continue to reduce the supply of allowances. This means less supply, more demand with ETS 2, and likely longer term price rises in EU allowances.

Risks

Other than the political risks of changing the supply/demand balance of allowances, with energy prices rising, and Middle East energy supply falling due to the Iran war, energy production in the US and Europe is likely to rise to make up the shortfalls and this may increase emissions in these markets putting upward pressure on carbon prices. The flipside risk is that renewable energy gets a boost, as now natural gas from Qatar is seen as less reliable and with gas prices rising, renewable power becomes relatively more attractive, and with renewables comes lower emissions.

The Expense Ratio

KRBN's expense ratio at 0.90% is admittedly high, especially compared to the ETF median of 0.5%. However, KRBN is the only ETF I can find that is purely exposed to carbon allowances, so there is an element of scarcity pricing for this expense ratio.

KRBN's Nice Yield

Because KRBN invests in futures, and futures are leveraged, KRBN holds collateral. As an example, if a futures contract has a price of $100, and requires only a 10% margin, the long futures holder would pay $10, and keep $90. So in the case of KRBN, it puts that non-margin funding into short-duration low-risk investments, generating a yield of around money-market rates. Most of this for KRBN is invested in the KraneShares Sustainable Ultra Short Duration Index ETF​, which tracks the Solactive ISS Sustainable Select 0-1 Year USD Corporate IG Index. This has a yield of just over 4% currently, and makes up 62% of KRBN's net asset value. Other collateral is held in US dollar cash and equivalents, including cash in margin accounts, which will usually pay interest at short-term money market rates.

This enables KRBN to pay a dividend, even when commodities carbon allowances and carbon allowance futures don't produce any yield. This is the advantage of buying futures, relative to holding physical allowances.

Conclusions

Overall short-term sentiment on the S&P Global Carbon webinar was cautious. The analysts noted that prices have fallen recently across most markets because of delays in policy decisions. In 2026 we may even see a price cap in EU ETS, which would limit the upside potential of KRBN, in the short term. But longer term, given the system’s effectiveness it's likely any softening will be temporary.

So I am quite bullish about the longer-term developments as carbon allowance supply tightens and regulations are getting stricter in most markets, putting upward pressure on carbon prices over time. For investors whose portfolio companies have a large carbon footprint, hydrocarbon producers, thermal power, aviation, etc, a small position in KRBN is an excellent way to hedge the carbon costs and price exposure that lie within. If you understand the regulatory policies well and have a strong view on that, then KRBN could be a more speculative instrument. But for me, holding KRBN is for portfolio risk reduction and diversification.

data.title: When The Markets Finally Go Off 'Autopilot'
data.description:
The markets trade at extreme valuations based on most historical metrics such as a Shiller PE, even after a bit of 'hiccup' to start 2026 largely due to regional war in the Middle East. That said, equities have maintained these extreme valuations for some time now.

It is important to note equities have had some key tailwinds throughout the past several decades, albeit with some major selloffs from time to time. In today's column, I will not focus on the short to near-term factors that could influence the markets. This article will highlight longer term tailwinds for equities that will likely start to turn to headwinds in the coming years. These trends are getting little mention in the financial press currently. However, they will likely reduce market returns for the foreseeable future.

It is important to note some of the key factors that have boosted equities for decades. We will start with 1978, when legislation was passed establishing the 401K. This change not only 'democratized' the market for retail investors, it enabled brought a huge new source of demand for equities. 401Ks accounted for roughly $10 trillion of the $13.9 trillion of defined contribution assets as of the end of 3Q2025. The rest is mainly in IRAs.

This also set the stage for the huge gains in the markets in the 1980s, 1990s and beyond. 401Ks came into being when the PE on the S&P 500 was in the high single digits. It also was when the Baby Boomers were just coming into their prime earning years.

Then in 2006, the Pension Protection Act was passed. Among many things, this legislation made index funds the legal default for automatic contributions. These have been the primary drivers of passive investing moving from just over 5% of assets under management in 1996 to around 60% now. And these funds flow into stocks based on market cap weighting. Which means the top 10 largest stocks like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Alphabet (GOOG) receive roughly 40% of these large inflows. This is one factor behind the huge outperformance of the Magnificent Seven from 2023-2025.

However, the oldest Baby Boomer hits 80 years old in 2026 and the median age of this massive generational cohort is 71 years old. They hold the largest share of wealth in America, and they are passing from this earth in increasing numbers. Required mandatory distributions or RMDs from retirement accounts start at 73 years old. Behind increasing RMDs as this population continues to age and portfolios that will get liquidated to satisfy inheritances, this long-term tailwind will increasingly turn into a headwind for U.S. equities in the years ahead.

Then we have the impacts of company stock buybacks. Global stock buybacks hit just over $1.3 trillion in 2024. This was roughly equal all dividend payouts to put in perspective. Just under $950 billion of this total emanated from the U.S. with 2025 stock buybacks pegged at $1.2 trillion.

The challenge is many of these companies are AI-related and devoted much more of their free cash flow and balance sheet to funding massive AI infrastructure spending. This means less money available for stock buybacks in 2026 and likely beyond. Most of the rest are financial institutions which will have to curtail some stock repurchases if they need to increase credit loss provisions. Given the growing problems in private credit and commercial real estate, this seems likely.

Investors certainly have several near-term concerns that need to be resolved before equities have a chance to move significantly higher. Among the top two, is the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and some sort of ending to the regional war in the Middle East. New 'cockroaches' need to quit emerging from the private credit markets as well.

However, prudent investors should remain aware on the longer-term as well. And some key long-term tailwinds that have helped pushed equity valuations to historical high levels over the last several decades will start to become headwinds in the years ahead. It should be noted that back in late 2024, the equity strategy team at Goldman Sachs was predicting just three percent annual returns from the U.S. stock market over the next decade due primarily to valuations. They also put a 72% probability that bonds would outperform stocks over that time. Valuations that have grown more dear since it should be noted.

data.title: What To Buy On This Potential Road To World War 3
data.description:
I do not use the phrase “World War 3” lightly. The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran had already entered its fourth week, Iran had rejected the latest U.S. proposal as “one-sided and unfair,” and the diplomacy is fragile at best. In moments like this, investors need a calm and rational framework.

Fifty years from now, I suspect this period may be remembered as a major turning point in modern history. I am not saying we are destined for an apocalyptic world war, but I do believe the odds of a much larger conflict are no longer remote enough to ignore.

Why Is There a Likelihood for World War 3?

When I use the phrase “World War 3,” I mean a war that starts in one theater and then pulls in more nations because too many vital interests are exposed at once. The United States and Israel have been striking, Iran has kept retaliating, Gulf states have come under fire, oil traffic through Hormuz has been disrupted, and Western missile demand is now being split across the Middle East and Ukraine.

The core terms everyone wants do not fit together. Washington wants Iran to stop enriching uranium, surrender its enriched stockpile, curb its missile program, and cut support for Hezbollah. Tehran wants a formal end to the war, guarantees against future attacks, compensation, recognition of its position on Hormuz, and Lebanon folded into any ceasefire. Israel wants the freedom to keep striking pre-emptively and keep dismantling Iranian military infrastructure.

Each side also has a reason to think more fighting could improve its current position. Iran has long treated its nuclear program, missiles, and regional allies as the core of its deterrent after years of sanctions and sabotage. Giving them up would likely be an unacceptable concession. Israel treats those same capabilities as the reason any pause of fighting would be temporary. Washington has tied its diplomacy to rolling back those capabilities, so it cannot easily settle for a ceasefire without those concessions from Iran. Iran hopes pressure on oil and shipping weakens Western resolve. Israel hopes more strikes leave Iran with fewer options. The U.S. hopes coercion produces concessions. This does not seem to produce any shared destination.

The Strait of Hormuz’s importance in the global oil trade is what can turn this from a regional war into a global one. Europe, China, India, Japan, and every fuel-importing economy all inherit a direct stake in the war. Every extra front adds another country, another military, another shipping lane, and another chance for some accident that forces a wider response.

There is another escalation path that is darker than merely oil. Iran threatened to hit Gulf desalination plants if the U.S. struck its power grid. The fact that desalination was even on the table tells you how extreme the escalation ladder already is. Bahrain and Qatar get 100% of their water from desalination, the UAE gets more than 80% of its drinking water that way, and Saudi Arabia gets about half its water supply from it. A sustained attack on desalination infrastructure could likely make much of the GCC totally uninhabitable, causing the most extreme humanitarian crisis in our lifetimes. This can also become a basis for much more extensive involvement by other nations.

I hesitate to make a total world war my base case. States usually stop short of max escalation when the costs become obvious. But this is no longer a scenario that can be dismissed as pure fantasy. The conflict already has the ingredients that make wars spread: incompatible war aims, weak diplomacy, and tightly interlocked dependencies and alliances. That mix is quite rare and overall not something anyone alive today has really dealt with because the post-Bretton Woods, U.S.-led world order has been relatively stable for almost 80 years.

The market seems very split on what to make of the whole conflict. I think the market almost needs to believe that the situation will buff out after some time. There is the idea of the “TACO trade” that was popularized last year with all the tariffs (TACO is short for “Trump Always Chickens Out”, which sort of makes fun of President Trump’s approach to foreign policy).

Here is what Polymarket thinks about Iran. As you can see, these markets have quite a bit of volume:

Basically, the chance for a U.S. ground invasion and a ceasefire by year end are both over 50%. Perhaps the market thinks an imminent ground invasion will cause Iran to surrender and agree to a ceasefire? Yet the last time the U.S. was in the Middle East, it was there for 20 years and the fighting was continuous.

How To Invest In Unprecedented Times?

The first step is recognizing that there is nothing new under the sun. It is true that no one alive today has lived through a true world war as an investor. But human beings have lived through currency breakdowns, energy shocks, rationing, confiscation, and mass capital destruction. In those instances, there were always some things that worked.

When you study that history, the conclusion is always that commodities deserve the first look, and gold deserves the first look among commodities. Commodities are inputs and bearer assets. One does not need an organizational structure like a corporation or government to give the asset value. The asset has value because it is an input for economic production. In the case of gold, its scarcity and durability and history is enough.

Gold has a very long record as a store of value, central banks have bought more than 1,000 tonnes in each of the last three years. De-dollarization following the sanctions against Russia is widely regarded as a reason for this shift.

Gold has had a really great three years and could have entered oversold territory. In the four days after the first U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, gold actually fell nearly 4%. This is something to consider. Overall, gold’s pristine track record of surviving basically everything in human history means that this is the top choice. Currently it is down almost 20% from highs. The drop could have momentum that may in the near term override gold’s normal safe haven behavior.

After gold, we move to cash and cash-adjacent instruments. In widespread and very large-scale war, cash from the wrong government can become a problem (meaning it goes to zero—think German marks after WWI), but cash from the right government is still a clean defense against forced capital destruction. The trade-off however is that you lock in a nearly guaranteed negative real return, but you preserve optionality to buy bargains, assuming you picked the right currency. My assumption is that the U.S. will still be okay, so the dollar would be the currency I am talking about keeping as cash.

One step up from plain USD is short duration credit. FLOT tracks U.S. dollar investment-grade floating rate bonds with maturities from one month to five years. This ETF holds over 500 corporate bonds and has an expense ratio of 0.15%, a 30-day SEC yield of 4.04%, and an effective duration of 0.01 years. This is decent when inflation can re-accelerate and rate expectations can jump around. You still carry credit exposure, so it is not the same thing as T-bills, but it is a very reasonable place to sit if your main objective is survival with some barely positive real yield.

BOXX is the more interesting cash alternative. Its objective is to match or exceed the 1-3 month Treasury bill market, and it does that through listed options combinations called box spreads. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.19%. I like BOXX a lot because it actually does a clever tax trick to turn all the “yield” into share price appreciation, which defers the income until you sell the shares. On an after tax basis, BOXX should outperform FLOT even though the yield on box spreads should be less than the yield on floating rate corporate bonds. I wrote a pretty thorough article on BOXX here.

I think Bitcoin is worth considering too. I am a Bitcoin bull, but I understand it's controversial. Bitcoin is a commodity. The value of BTC does not depend on future cash flows but rather on the robustness of the digital ledger that is the Bitcoin blockchain. In a global conflict, the problem with many traditional assets is that their future cash flows become much harder to trust. Bitcoin does not have this problem. Its ledger will be robust as long as the Internet is around. In times of massive capital destruction, a digital ledger that no one can unilaterally change that creates a scarce monetary unit may see a meaningful bid. Consider again that the only other options seem to be gold (which would be very hard to move around and require custodians) and cash and cash equivalents (which is pretty much guaranteed negative or zero real returns due to guaranteed inflation caused by war). A digital bearer asset that is scarce and neutral could be quite useful as a means for storing and moving value.

If I had to pick one equity ETF for an Iran conflict that spirals into something much larger, I would pickSPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR). I thought hard about energy equities, because oil was the first big mover in this war. But oil can fall fast on diplomacy, waivers, or a shipping normalization. Rearmament tends to last longer. NATO said European allies and Canada lifted defense spending by 20% in real terms in 2025, and MBDA expects a 40% jump in production this year because missile demand is straining inventories. Even if Iran and Israel and the U.S. all became friends tomorrow, I think the entire ordeal would have caused leaders worldwide to be more vigilant in being prepared for big conflict, and this process could be a long-term tailwind for defense stocks. XAR tracks a modified equal-weighted aerospace and defense index, holds 41 names, and charges 0.35%. The truth is, the logic going into XAR is very uncomplicated and unremarkable. I am just looking at which equities have the clearest claim on a world where governments need more ships, more interceptors, more missiles, and more manufacturing capacity than they thought 1-3 months ago. Then I would think about having a broad basket of it. That’s it. Again, I don’t think it makes sense to be taking on equity risk premia because world war will almost certainly destroy much capital on a global scale.

Conclusion

My base case is still that the world avoids the worst outcome. I do not think investors should build an apocalypse portfolio and wait for the sky to fall. But investors also make a mistake when they assume every geopolitical fire will burn out on schedule. The stakes are quite high now and owning more bearer assets and less assets that depend on multi-national cooperation seems to be the smarter choice.

I would put gold as number one. FLOT and BOXX are great positions for a serious risk-off view. Only consider BTC if you could tolerate the volatility and really understand the BTC thesis of it being a unique neutral bearer asset that happens to be digital.

Don’t bet on the end of the world. Even if you win, it really would not matter. But do consider bets that the world is going to be more dangerous, because that probability is now higher than before.

This too will pass.

data.title: Carvana's Mounting Headwinds Warrant Caution - Maintain Hold
data.description:
I previously covered Carvana Co. (CVNA) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Hold rating then, with the stock's expensive valuations negating the richer profit margins and the healthier balance sheet.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold rating for the CVNA stock, with the ongoing selloff yet to trigger a compelling entry point, attributed to the numerous macro and execution headwinds likely to trigger further margin compression risks.

CVNA Faces Numerous Headwinds Entering 2026

Since my last Hold rating, CVNA has proven to be extremely volatile through the stock's rally to a new 52 week high of $486s and the consequent meltdown to the June/November 2025 trading floor of $290s by the time of writing - all of which presenting the risks tied to chasing overpriced and highly shorted stocks.

1. Higher Inflationary Pressure

For one, I am of the opinion that the macroeconomic outlook remains mixed in the intermediate term, as observed in the slightly hotter US CPI in February 2026.

This is worsened by the emergence of the K-shaped economy, with the spending divergence between the higher income and the lower income consumers likely to further widen.

The ongoing Iran conflict has also contributed to the rising fuel prices to $3.925 per gallon as of March 21, 2026 (compared $2.896 per gallon in December 2025 and $2.61 per gallon in 2019), with it remaining to be seen when things may be concluded.

As a result of these events, I am of the opinion that March 2026 may bring forth a higher inflationary pressure, as traders also price in the higher for longer oil prices through the end of the year at up to $180 per barrel compared to the $56s observed in 2019.

These development matters indeed, since it may temporarily impact the demand for CVNA's used ICE vehicle sales, worsened by the prior EV demand destruction, with it naturally contributing to the pessimism surrounding the used vehicle dealers'/marketplace's prospects.

2. Affordability Risks

The same inflationary pressure has also been observed in the increasingly higher used vehicle prices at $40.78K in Q4'25 compared to the $20.61K reported in 2019, with it naturally contributing to the higher average used car payments at $532 per month in Q3'25 compared to the $388 reported in 2019 (author estimated).

The same has been observed in the borrowing costs for used cars at elevated APR of 10.9% in February 2026, compared to the 8.2% observed in December 2019, as some (albeit a small group of) analysts already price a potential Fed rate hike in 2026.

Combined with the US consumers already taking on longer car loan terms beyond the typical 46/60 months to 72/100 months and the growing auto loan balance at $1.66T in FQ4'25 (+25.5% from FQ4'19 levels), I am of the opinion that the auto sector face affordability risks ahead.

3. Margin Risks

As a result of the uncertain macroeconomic environment, it remains to be seen if CVNA may be able to sustain their outsized FQ4'25 performance entering FQ2'26, with:

  • the retail units sold at 163.52K in FQ4'25 (+43% YoY),
  • the revenues of $5.6B (+58% YoY), and
  • the estimated retail unit pricing at $34.26K (+10.4% YoY).

This is despite the used vehicle seller likely to report a robust FQ1'26 sales performance, as observed in the Cox Automotive already reporting:

  • a healthy Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index [MUVVI] at 213.4x for the first 15 days for March 2026 (+0.5% MoM and +5.3% YoY),
  • the higher prices by +5.3% YoY, attributed to pricing growth and the outperforming luxury segment, and
  • the higher wholesale used vehicle sales outpacing supply through the first half of March 2026,

with it remaining to be seen if things may be sustained in Q2'26.

This is especially since CVNA already reports a mixed adj EBITDA margin of 9.1% in FQ4'25 (-1 points YoY), attributed to the higher reconditioning costs and retail depreciation rates, as similarly observed in:

  • the mixed Retail vehicle gross profit of $3.31K per unit in FY2025 (+0.1% YoY) and
  • the wholesale gross profit of $806 per unit (-3% YoY),

all of which already triggering their decelerating total gross profit growth to $7.02K per unit (+1.7% YoY), compared to a year ago at +25.3% YoY.

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to the deflation from the management's "decision to give back to customers in the form of lower interest rates" to seemingly drive higher retail/wholesale unit sales at +45.1% YoY in FY2025, compared to a year ago at +31.2% YoY.

As a result, while CVNA may have reported notable "improvements in cost of funds and higher finance and VSC attach rates," ie: extended warranty, it is apparent that their margin performance is mixed at best, with it perhaps explaining the stock's steep meltdown from the 52 week highs.

This is especially since the management has expected the margin headwind to continue for a little longer for up to 6 months, albeit with "a sequential increase in retail GPU in Q1," with it underscoring their likely to be mixed results ahead.

4. Premium Valuations Meet Mixed Margins

For now, CVNA has offered a promising FY2026 guidance with higher retail units sold and higher adj EBITDA, with it building upon the +43.3% YoY and +62.7% YoY observed in FY2025, respectively.

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain excellent, with the company expected to report a robust top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +25.6%/+17.3% through FY2028, compared to the historical top-line growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +29.5%.

Thanks to the -42.2% correction from the 52 weeks high, CVNA's FWD EV/EBITDA has moderated to 15.71x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 39.82x from the recent highs of 25.89x/84.93x, albeit still notably elevated compared to the sector median of 9.30x/14.41x, respectively.

The same conclusion may be observed in my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.30x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 39.82x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +17.3%, against the sector median of 1.44x.

When comparing CVNA's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 15.71x, FY2025 adj EBITDA margins of 10.8%, and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.30x to its direct competitors, including:

  • CarGurus (CARG) at 9.63x/35% in FY2025/0.78x,
  • Cars.com (CARS) at 4.17x/29.1% in FY2025/0.53x, and
  • CarMax (KMX) at 25.09x/14% on a YTD basis/2.18x, respectively,

it is apparent that CVNA remains expensive despite the recent meltdown, attributed to the underwhelming adj EBITDA profit margins and adj EPS growth prospects, with a similar conclusion also derived based on the management's long-term adj EBITDA margin guidance of 13.5% (by 2030-2035).

Part of the premium valuations may be attributed to CVNA's notably healthier balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 1.22x in FY2025, compared to the Auto & Truck Dealerships averages of 4.06x.

Assuming a sustained prudence in their debt leveraging, I am of the opinion that the company is likely to remain resilient during the challenging next few months/quarters ahead.

CVNA Remains A Hold Here

For now, CVNA has lost much of their recent gains albeit seemingly retesting the established uptrend support line since late 2023, with it remaining to be seen if the bulls may defend the $280s before the stock's potential upward recovery as per historical trends.

The same bottoming has been seemingly observed in CVNA's oversold technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes, with it offering traders a great insight into its intermediate term recovery prospects, if the bulls defend the stock's historical trading pattern.

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that there remains a mixed risk/reward profile here, given the notable gap to my base-case fair value estimates of $81.80, based on the sector P/E median of 14.41x and the FY2025 adj EPS performance of $5.68 (+257% YoY) after excluding the non cash benefit of $618M.

The same may be observed against my base-case long-term price target of $185.80, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $12.90, with it apparent that CVNA remains extremely expensive despite the recent selloff while lending credence to the market's pessimism after the recent earnings call, where "a miss for a high multiple growth stock is not a good combo."

Combined with the likely to remain elevated reconditioning costs and retail depreciation rates, I am of the opinion that CVNA's near-term performance metrics are likely to disappoint before potentially recovering in H2'26.

As a result of the ongoing macroeconomic/geopolitical uncertainties, I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to at best trade sideways pending any recovery catalysts, with it implying its mixed return prospects in the near-term, worsened by the potential volatility from the still elevated short interest ratio of 9.27%.

CVNA is at best a Hold here.

data.title: Perma-Fix Set To Do Much Better In H2 '26
data.description:
Perma-Fix Environmental Services, Inc. (PESI), the waste management company we've been following for years, did produce disappointing Q4 results. Apart from Q1, a better FY26 lies ahead as the company's fortunes are boosted from Hanford (the biggest nuclear waste site in the U.S.), international opportunities, and its PFAS removal business. Let's start with the biggest opportunity, Hanford, where waste removal (grouting) has finally started.

Hanford

After years of delay, the DFLAW grouting plant finally started in October 2025 and has processed approximately 50K gallons of tank waste through February 2026. Management expects revenue of $1M to $2M per month from DFLAW-related activities starting in Q2/26, ramping up throughout the year. And there are additional opportunities (Q4CC):

In addition to supporting DFLAW program, we expect to participate in several other Hanford related waste streams and site programs that will generate additional treatment demand over time. These increases in receipts include providing solidification, treatment support to high-volume contaminated water from the Hanford site as well as increasing our Transuranic Waste Processing program by 100% beginning this month, supported by additional shifts at the Perma-Fix Northwest facility. As waste receipts increase, we expect to utilize the expanded capacity to support a growing volume of treatment activity tied to both Hanford Mission and other DOE programs.

DOE plans to use PESI’s Northwest facility to grout effluent (blowdown water) rather than vitrify it, thereby providing another 20% or so of the waste volume for treatment.

The DOE has increased targets to treat up to 200M gallons of Hanford tank waste through 2040, with a supplemental grouting program set to begin in 2026.

A significant long-term opportunity exists via a recent RFP to treat 22 tanks (containing up to 50M gallons) through grouting, starting in January 2028, a contract valued at approximately $4B.

PFAS

PESI's Generation 2.0 PFAS Destruction System at Oak Ridge triples capacity to 3K gallons per day and is nearly complete. Testing is scheduled for late April or early May 2026. PESI's PFAS destruction solution is significantly cheaper compared to its main alternative, incineration, and the process is emission-free.

Management aims for incremental margins for PFAS treatment to be between 60% and 70%, with prices from $10 to $15 per gallon for large volumes. Prices can exceed $30 per gallon for smaller quantities.

Treatment Segment

FY25 revenue increased 29% y/y to $10.1M, with both volumes and prices contributing, from the Q4CC:

Operationally, our Treatment segments delivered meaningful improvement during the year. We saw higher waste volumes, improved plant throughput and stronger waste mix.

The waste treatment backlog grew by 51% y/y to $11.9M as of year-end 2025, and things are likely to improve further (Q4CC, our emphasis):

One of the most significant milestones in the year was the renewal of the permit for our Perma-Fix Northwest facility. This permit significantly expands our permitted processing capacity to approximately 1.2 million gallons of liquid mixed waste annually, effectively tripling our liquid processing capacity and also authorizes treatment of up to 175,000 tons of waste through macro encapsulation annually. Combined with our investments in automation, facility upgrades and workforce expansion, these improvements meaningfully strengthen the role Perma-Fix Northwest can play in support of multiple Hanford-related waste streams and other DOE emission objectives as activity ramps in the coming quarters.

Management argues that much of the H1/26 growth in the treatment segment will mostly come from treating TRU (transuranic) waste. TRU removal is a specialized and high-margin treatment business for PESI.

TRU waste consists of materials (debris, soil, or sludge) contaminated with elements heavier than uranium, such as plutonium, typically originating from nuclear weapons research and production.

PESI has the only commercial facility in the U.S. authorized by the DOE to treat TRU waste. The company is doubling its TRU waste capacity at the Richland facility, adding additional shifts.

As a side note, this capacity can also be used for Hanford, where there is a huge storage of TRU waste waiting to be processed.

Services Segment

Revenue was down $7.6M for FY25 due to timing issues mostly related to the government shutdown.

However, the segment gained over $30M in new backlog and submitted over $40M in new bids during Q1/26. New awards include a radiological facility demolition project at a National Lab and three new field project mobilizations in March 2026.

And just in, the company won a $24M contract with Lawrence Livermore National Security, from the PR:

Perma-Fix will provide demolition, excavation, and waste management services to support the safe disposition of these facilities as part of site efforts aligned with LLNL’s national security mission. The project is expected to draw on Perma-Fix’s technical expertise in complex radiological and hazardous waste handling, facility decontamination, and nuclear waste management.

It's a two-year contract, but it doesn't state when the work begins, and PESI will operate with 3 local subcontractors, but this contract alone goes a long way to put the Services Segment back on its feet.

International Operations

International revenue surged 163% y/y to $6.4M in 2025. Some of the main developments here:

  • A new liquid treatment project in Canada, starting in April, with additional projects starting in Q3.
  • An Italian project involving drum characterization with fieldwork starting in April, with waste receipts expected in 2027.
  • Waste streams continue from Germany, and additional work in Mexico is expected to be opened up for bids in late 2026, from the Q4CC:

The Mexico waste we did last year, there'll be another tranche of that out for bid here. It's already been out for bid. That won't likely get rolling probably until Q3 or Q4.

Finances

  • Q4 revenue was up 6.9% to $15.72M, missing analyst estimates by $2M.
  • GAAP EPS of -$0.15 missed estimates by $0.06. The net loss for FY25 was $13.8M, an improvement from a $20M loss in FY24.
  • Management views Q2/26 as a major inflection point, with significant waste receipts from Hanford and new service projects expected to drive a return to profitability.
  • The company had $11.8M in cash, down from $29M the previous year, following heavy investment in capital projects like the PFAS reactors. The company had $1.8M in debt.

Outlook

  • Management expects a soft Q1 with losses exceeding $4M in negative EBITDA on roughly $13M in revenue. This is attributed to seasonal field activity lulls and the shifting of $2M in revenue from Q1 to Q2 due to revenue recognition rules.
  • Management will submit a permit modification to increase the Northwest facility's liquid capacity by an additional 3M gallons (reaching a total of 4.2M gallons) to accommodate the anticipated scale of Hanford tank waste.
  • Increased throughput in the coming quarters is expected to better absorb fixed costs and achieve meaningful margin improvement.

Valuation

At $11 per share, the market cap is $203.7M and an EV of $194.2M. With analysts expecting FY26 revenue at $103.5M, that delivers an EV/S of almost 1.9x.

Analysts expect EPS to come in at $0.33 this year but rise steeply next year to $1.15 as Hanford and PFAS scale.

Conclusion

Just as we like cyclical stocks in a downturn, we like growth companies experiencing temporary headwinds. PESI suffered from some temporary headwinds that will be over by the end of Q1, that is, in two weeks.

With Hanford and the PFAS business scaling, management promises us an inflection point and a return to profitability in Q2. International business is doing very well also, so we think getting in at $11 (or adding) offers excellent risk/reward.

The main risk is further unforeseen headwinds, perhaps from a prolonged economic fallout from the crisis in the Middle East, but it seems to us that much of its business (most notably Hanford) isn't really sensitive to what happens in the global economy.

data.title: BUI Rights Offering Strategy
data.description:
BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust's (BUI), a utilities/infrastructure-focused closed-end fund, rights offering is live. The Worker's Advocate, a contributing author to the Income Lab, recently covered BUI more generally for readers here. This article will specifically focus on the mechanics of the ongoing rights offering and the choices that investors in the fund have.

From the press release:

BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI) (the "Fund") today announced the terms of transferable rights ("Rights") to be issued to the holders of the Fund's common shares of beneficial interest (par value $0.001 per share) ("Shares") as of March 9, 2026 (the "Record Date"). Rights entitle the holder to purchase one new Share for every 4 Rights exercised (1-for-4). The subscription price per Share (the "Subscription Price") will be determined on the expiration date of the Offer, which is currently expected to be April 2, 2026, unless extended by the Fund (the "Expiration Date"), and will be equal to 95% of the average of the last reported sales price per Share on the New York Stock Exchange (the "NYSE") on the Expiration Date and each of the four (4) immediately preceding trading days provided that, if such price is equal to or above net asset value (“NAV”) at the Expiration Date, it shall be reduced to $0.01 below NAV on the Expiration Date (the "Formula Price"). If, however, the Formula Price is less than 95% of the Fund's NAV per Share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the Expiration Date, the Subscription Price will be 95% of the Fund's NAV per Share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the Expiration Date. Rights are transferable and are expected to be admitted for trading on the NYSE under the symbol "BUI RT" during the course of the Offer and will cease trading April 1, 2026, one trading day before the Expiration Date.

The ex-rights date was March 9, 2026, while the expiry date is April 2, 2026. This is a transferable 1-for-4 offering with an interesting subscription formula. If the fund trades at a premium at expiry, the subscription price will be $0.01 below NAV (effectively par). However, if the fund is at a discount, the -5% discount floor will immediately be hit. What this means is that the subscription discount will effectively remain within a narrow range of 0% to -5%.

For rights holders, subscribing is beneficial as long as the fund is trading above a -5% discount at expiration. What is interesting is that unlike typical rights offerings that use a "95% of market price" formula (capping the subscription discount at around 5% to the market price no matter how high the premium gets), BUI's formula makes the benefit increase as the premium expands because the subscription price is anchored at NAV. In other words, the higher the premium at expiry, the larger the gain from subscribing. On the other hand, investors should not subscribe if the fund ends below a -5% discount at expiry, as it would be cheaper to purchase shares on the open market.

Interestingly, BUI's premium continued rising after the rights offering was announced, bucking the usual trend of the post-announcement selloff.

That premium status made the rights intrinsically valuable after issuance, and they traded up to around $0.10 shortly after the ex-rights date. However, the premium then quickly flipped to a discount, driving both the share price and the rights price down.

This again supports our "sell and rebuy" recommendation for tactical investors: sell upon announcement of the rights offering, either immediately or before the ex-rights date at the latest, and look to buy back at a lower price after the offering expires. However, note that this outcome is not guaranteed, and also tax issues are not considered.

Overall, this is one of the more shareholder-friendly rights offerings that have been conducted recently. The -5% discount floor limits dilution, as does the less aggressive 1-for-4 ratio (as opposed to the most typical 1-for-3).
