Even assuming maximum subscription, the projected NAV-per-share impact is only about 1%, and that estimate is after factoring in the fact that offering costs are being absorbed by the manager rather than charged to the fund.

Action plan

In summary, this is a basic action plan for BUI investors:

  1. Subscribe if:
    • The discount remains narrower than -5% at expiry.
    • You want to increase your position and believe in BUI long-term.
  2. Do NOT subscribe if:
    • The discount widens beyond -10% at expiry.
    • It would be cheaper to buy shares directly on the open market instead.
  3. If you don't want to subscribe:
    • Sell your rights on the open market, as the value of the rights may potentially decline as the offering period progresses.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: First BanCorp. Isn't Prime For A Downgrade Yet
data.description:
Whenever I rate a company a "Buy," I'm making the claim that shares should outperform the market for the foreseeable future. So far, that seems to be the case when it comes to First BanCorp. (FBP), a reasonably sized bank with a market capitalization today of $3.17 billion. Back in late December of last year, I decided to upgrade the company from a "Hold" to a soft "Buy." This was in response to core deposits and asset quality improving. The rise in the company's net interest margin was encouraging, and shares were priced at levels that were not exactly great but that were low enough to justify a small amount of optimism.

Since that time, things have gone the way I had hoped they would. The stock is up 0.8%, while the S&P 500 is down 2.3%. That's not a huge degree of outperformance. But it is outperformance all the same. Looking at the newest data that management has offered up, I would say that my bullish stance remains solid. The company continues to see improvement in key areas, though it's not exactly the cheapest prospect out there. While there are cheaper firms available, very few of them have the kind of asset quality that this institution does. So for now, I think that maintaining it as a soft "Buy" is appropriate here.

The picture continues to improve

The final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year saw deposits for First BanCorp. come in at $16.67 billion. That happens to be down from the $16.87 billion that the company reported a year earlier. This on its own is disappointing to say the least. That's especially true when you consider that, during this window of time, high-cost brokered deposits ended up climbing from $478.1 million to $593.6 million over the last year. This means that actual organic deposits for the company declined.

Usually when I see deposits drop like this, especially at a time when brokered deposits are rising, I become discouraged. Deposits are the literal fuel for banks that are used for growth. However, management also has a category of deposits that it calls core deposits. These exclude brokered deposits and government deposits. Broker deposits are borrowed from other financial institutions and are not always reliable. And government deposits, such as the $2.5 billion that the company has from the government of Puerto Rico specifically, can fluctuate based on budgetary decisions and other factors. So looking at deposits and adjusting for these is a wise idea. Following this approach, year over year, core deposits for the company actually grew from $12.9 billion to $13.1 billion. It is worth mentioning that about 28.8% of the company’s deposits are classified as uninsured. This falls just under the 30% maximum that I typically like to see.

At the end of 2025, First BanCorp. had loans totaling $12.89 billion. This compares to the $12.52 billion that the company reported a year earlier. As has historically been the case, the largest chunk of this loan base is concentrated in Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands. In total, those loans amounted to $10.63 billion. Of that, $3.70 billion involves consumer loans, while $2.52 billion is tied up in commercial and industrial loans. The company also has meaningful exposure when it comes to residential mortgages and commercial mortgages, on top of about $2.50 billion of loans invested in Florida.

We should not ignore other parts of the balance sheet as well. For instance, the company has about $4.86 billion invested in securities. That is down slightly from the $4.93 billion reported a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $1.16 billion down to $658.6 million. And finally, debt plummeted from $561.7 million to $290 million. I know it may seem like I am just giving you a data dump. However, all of these data points paint a very interesting picture. When taken together, you see a company that is reducing leverage, cutting down on securities, and prioritizing high-value core deposits, all with the aim of boosting its loan portfolio. This has a significant positive impact on overall revenue and profitability.

In the chart above, you can see key income statement data for the institution. The final quarter of 2025 saw net interest income of $199.8 million. That's up from the $188.4 million reported a year earlier. This was even though the provision for credit losses for the institution grew from $20.9 million to $23 million. And a lot of it stems from the fact that management succeeded, thanks to the changes in its balance sheet, in pushing up net interest margin from 4.33% to 4.68%.

This metric improved not only because of the changes in the balance sheet composition but also because interest rates have allowed the company to pay depositors less than they were paying before. For instance, time deposits went from costing the company 4.83% per annum in 2024 to costing 4.27% last year. This is because payments being made to depositors can be scaled down immediately as interest rates get cut. However, some aspects of the balance sheet remain locked in at certain rates for an extended window of time. For instance, when it comes to the loans on its books, $7.49 billion worth is locked in at fixed interest rates due more than one year out from the end of the latest quarter. What's more, a hefty $3.38 billion of loans do not mature until more than five years out, with $1.22 billion maturing after 15 years out. This means that the company will be able to continue to benefit from a spread in interest rates for some time, though there will naturally be some refinancing activity that goes on here.

Non-interest income for the company has improved slightly, growing from $32.2 million in the final quarter of 2024 to $34.4 million in the final quarter of 2025. But that's not a substantial increase. Still, it did help to push net profits up from $75.7 million to $87.1 million. And for the year as a whole, the $344.9 million in net income generated by the company surpassed the $298.7 million reported the year before.

If we use the data provided by management for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced relative to earnings. It is actually trading cheaper than it was before, with a price-to-earnings multiple of 9.2. That is below the 10 maximum that I typically look for. It also makes the company tied as the cheapest between it and five other banks that I compared it to in that chart. Unfortunately, the picture is a little different when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In the chart below, you can see that four of the five competitors are cheaper than it is on a price-to-book basis. And on a price-to-tangible-book basis, two of them are cheaper.

Normally, the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples would be considered quite lofty to me. However, one really positive thing about the institution is that asset quality is robust. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.81%. I typically like to see a reading here of 1% or more. This actually puts the company comfortably above any of the five banks that I compared it to. It is ranked similarly when it comes to its return on equity reading of 17.84%, shown in the subsequent chart below. That is meaningfully higher than the 10% minimum that I look for.

One thing that I would like to see some progress on would be the non-performing assets of the institution. Typically, I like no more than 0.50% of all assets to be classified as non-performing. But at the end of 2025, this number was just a bit higher at 0.60%. Similarly, 0.70% of its loans were classified as non-accrual. This is also a bit above what I generally like to see. But the good news is that this number and the preceding number were both about the same a year earlier. So at least we aren't seeing any deterioration here.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that First BanCorp. remains a decently attractive prospect. It is certainly not a home run candidate. However, it is one that, thanks to high quality and continued improvement that management is making, justifies a soft "Buy" rating at this time.
